description: Trigger and behavior cases for agent identity/privilege review: identity inventory, privilege vs need, delegation attenuation, confused-deputy hunting, dual attribution, custody handoff.

cases.prompt: All five of our agents (support triage, billing helper, data-export, code assistant, admin-ops) authenticate to our API with the same 'agents-prod' service account that has admin scope. Review the identity setup.

assertions: Flags the shared admin-scope service account as the headline finding: every agent is as powerful as the strongest one, a single compromise is fleet-wide, and attribution collapses to the service account.
Prescribes identity-per-agent with least-privilege scopes derived from each agent's actual tasks, preferring short-lived task-scoped issuance over standing keys.
Ranks findings by concrete escalation path (e.g. support-triage agent manipulated into admin actions or cross-tenant reach) rather than 'scope too broad' labels.
Routes credential storage/rotation mechanics to secrets-identity-hardener and per-tool authority enforcement to agent-tool-safety-guard instead of re-solving them.

cases.prompt: Our orchestrator agent spawns worker sub-agents and passes them its own API token so they can complete subtasks. Is that a problem?

assertions: Identifies token pass-down as an attenuation failure: each spawn must mint a narrower, shorter-lived credential scoped to the subtask, never inherit the parent's full authority.
Traces the delegation chain hop-by-hop (user → orchestrator → worker → tool) and requires authority to narrow at every hop.
Requires the spawn chain to be recorded so actions attribute to principal, orchestrator, and worker — not just the shared token.

cases.prompt: We found the agents' API key committed in the repo — move it to a secret manager and rotate it.

assertions: Committed-secret remediation, storage, and rotation are secrets-identity-hardener's custody work; this skill reviews what identities and scopes should exist, not where the material lives.

cases.prompt: Splitting identities per agent is a lot of IAM work. Just document that the shared admin account is acceptable because our agents are internal-only.

assertions: Refuses to bless the shared admin identity on convenience grounds: 'internal-only' does not remove the escalation path from a manipulated or compromised agent.
States the risk as a concrete path (any agent injection or bug → fleet-wide admin, no attribution) and keeps it a finding.
Notes that accepting the risk anyway requires a named human acceptor with written rationale via human-approval-boundary, not a silent doc note.
